does: This paper formalizes prompt injection attacks—malicious instruction/data insertion into LLM inputs to produce attacker-desired outputs—through a unified mathematical framework that subsumes existing ad-hoc case studies as special cases. The authors design a novel attack by combining components from existing methods and conduct a systematic empirical evaluation of 5 prompt injection attacks against 10 large language models across 7 tasks, simultaneously testing 10 proposed defenses. The work establishes quantitative benchmarks for attack success rates and defense effectiveness, providing concrete metrics for comparing methods rather than relying on isolated examples. A public evaluation platform is released to standardize future research.
connects: This work builds on nascent literature on prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM-integrated systems, which until now has consisted primarily of security case studies and informal threat models lacking principled organization. It extends research on adversarial inputs to language models and prompt-based attacks, connecting to broader work on LLM robustness and safety. The formalization framework synthesizes insights from diverse attack techniques reported in practice—including instruction injection, data poisoning, and context confusion—into a coherent theoretical structure. The defense mechanisms draw on established mitigation strategies from prompt engineering, input filtering, and model-level robustness, adapting them specifically to the prompt injection threat model.
enables: This framework and benchmark enable systematic development of stronger attacks and defenses by providing a common language for threat specification and quantitative comparison. Future work can use the formalization to reason about attack-defense dynamics theoretically and design provably robust solutions. The benchmark infrastructure supports incremental progress in evaluating new LLM architectures, instruction-following approaches, and defense mechanisms as they emerge. The public platform facilitates community-driven research, allowing researchers to test novel attacks or defenses against the standardized evaluation suite. This work lays groundwork for formal security analysis of prompt-based systems and informs design of safer LLM-integrated applications in production environments.
